# Historical Skepticism Toward New Technologies

Every major technological innovation in recorded history has provoked fear, moral panic, and organized resistance. The pattern is so consistent that it functions almost as a law of cultural dynamics: a new tool emerges, critics warn it will degrade human capacities or destroy social bonds, society adopts it anyway, and the cycle repeats with the next invention.

The earliest well-documented case is Socrates' objection to writing itself. In Plato's *Phaedrus*, Socrates argues that the written word will produce "forgetfulness in the soul" by encouraging people to rely on external marks rather than cultivating internal memory. The irony is structural: Socrates' oral argument against writing survives only because Plato committed it to text. This recursive quality — the critic's objection preserved and transmitted by the very medium being criticized — reappears throughout the history of technological resistance.

The printing press generated analogous anxieties about cognitive overload. Conrad Gessner, the Swiss polymath, warned in the sixteenth century that the sheer volume of printed material would prove "confusing and harmful" to readers. Newspapers drew criticism for atomizing the communal experience of receiving information — where once a congregation heard news together from the pulpit, now individuals read alone, each absorbing a private and potentially divergent account of the world. The fear was not merely about the content but about the social architecture that the medium imposed.

The automobile provoked some of the most visceral backlash. In the years following World War I, *The New York Times* ran alarming coverage of "Motor Killings," and widely cited statistics showed that more Americans died in domestic car accidents in the four postwar years than had been killed in combat in France. The phonograph was accused of draining the vitality from live musical performance and degrading public taste. Television earned its epithets — "the boob tube," "the idiot box" — alongside serious accusations that it eroded community life, shortened attention spans, and normalized violence. These criticisms were not entirely wrong; the interesting thing is that partial correctness did not slow adoption. The internet and social media extended the pattern into the twenty-first century, drawing charges of addiction, misinformation, and social fragmentation that echo earlier complaints almost verbatim.

The term "Luddite," now shorthand for reflexive technophobia, originates in a more nuanced reality. The English textile workers who organized raids to destroy mechanized weaving frames in the 1810s were not ignorant of progress. Many likely understood that mechanization would eventually increase total wealth. Their objection was distributional: the transition would make their own lives worse in the near term, destroying livelihoods built around skills that machines rendered obsolete. The original Luddites grasped a tension that recurs with every technological wave — the gap between aggregate long-term benefit and concentrated short-term harm. This distinction between opposing a technology because it does not work and opposing it because the costs of its working fall unevenly is routinely collapsed in popular discourse, to the detriment of clear thinking.

Marshall McLuhan's observation that "we shape our tools and thereafter they shape us" captures the deeper dynamic. Technologies are not neutral instruments; they restructure perception, social organization, and economic relations. Each new tool serves as a mirror, reflecting both the aspirations and anxieties of the society that produces it. The critics are rarely entirely wrong about the costs. But the historical record is unambiguous on one point: adoption wins. Once a technology demonstrates sufficient utility, resistance delays but does not prevent its integration into daily life. The genie, as the saying goes, does not return to the bottle.

What varies across episodes is not whether resistance occurs but who bears the costs of transition, how long adjustment takes, and whether institutions manage the disruption or leave individuals to absorb it alone. The most productive responses to new technologies have historically come not from those who denied the technology's significance but from those who accepted its reality and fought over its governance — over who would benefit, who would be protected, and what new rules the reshaped world required.

## See Also

- [[z20260315-010|The Left's Intellectual Disengagement from AI and Its Political Consequences]] — grounds the recurring historical pattern of technological resistance in a contemporary case, where dismissal of AI's significance risks forfeiting influence over precisely the distributional questions that have mattered most in every prior technological transition
